did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses lightweight federated transfer learning models, specifically pre-trained deep learning architectures including EfficientNetV2S, EfficientNetB3, ResNet50, and NasNetMobile, to classify skin cancer types (dermatology task) from dermoscopic images. The study evaluates these AI models on both identically and non-identically distributed datasets within a federated learning ecosystem.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper proposes and develops a novel technique using lightweight federated transfer learning models (EfficientNetV2S, EfficientNetB3, ResNet50, NasNetMobile) for skin cancer detection. It presents original experimental results, including specific accuracy metrics (89.83% for IID, 90.64% for Non-IID), validation losses, and comparisons with state-of-the-art models, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Minimal sourced and lightweight federated transfer learning models for skin cancer detection
brief_summary: This study proposes a technique to classify skin cancer types using minimal resources and lightweight federated transfer learning models. The researchers applied pre-trained deep learning architectures, specifically EfficientNetV2S, EfficientNetB3, ResNet50, and NasNetMobile, to dermoscopic images resized to 32 × 32 × 3 pixels. The methodology involved implementing a federated learning ecosystem to evaluate model performance on both identically and non-identically distributed datasets. The main evaluation approach utilized accuracy, precision, recall, and categorical losses to assess the impact of low-pixel images across clients. The key result demonstrated that the minimal resource-based EfficientNetV2S achieved an accuracy of 89.83% for identically distributed data and 90.64% for non-identically distributed data. These findings suggest that lightweight models can effectively detect skin cancer while preserving data privacy through federated learning.

lesion_types_studied.details: The paper focuses on classifying skin cancer types. It explicitly mentions 'Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC)', 'Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC)', and 'Malignant Melanoma (MM)' as frequently documented skin carcinogenic cells. The study aims to classify 'type of skin cancer' using dermoscopic images, though specific class counts or dataset breakdowns for these individual types are not reported in the provided text.

The paper notes that 'nevus and melanoma lesions are so similar' causing diagnostic challenges. It mentions classifying lesions as 'nevus, melanoma' or 'melanoma, seborrhoeic keratosis, and nevus' in the context of related work and diagnostic difficulties, indicating these are relevant classes in the domain of the study's classification task.

Mentioned in the context of related work (Hosny et al.) where a softmax layer classifies segmented colour picture lesions as 'melanoma, seborrhoeic keratosis, and nevus'. It is part of the broader skin lesion classification landscape discussed.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer types (e.g., Squamous Cell Carcinoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Malignant Melanoma, Nevus)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly aims to 'classify type of skin cancer' using lightweight federated transfer learning models. It evaluates performance using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and categorical losses. The study compares models like EfficientNetV2S, EfficientNetB3, ResNet50, and NasNetMobile on dermoscopic images to predict discrete diagnostic categories of skin lesions.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A publicly available dataset of skin lesion images gathered from the Kaggle platform (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/andrewmv/isic-2019). It contains JPG-formatted dermoscopic images with proper labels.

use.training: The dataset was split into training and testing subsets using an 80:20 ratio. The training subset was used to aid the model in learning intricate details of the images.
use.testing: The testing subset was used to track model performance during training (by feeding it to the model at the conclusion of each epoch) and to evaluate the model's overall performance on new data after training was finished.
use.other: The data was randomly selected and distributed among N clients to simulate a federated learning environment with both identically distributed (IID) and non-identically distributed (Non-IID) scenarios.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To select the overall model by comparing the median model's performance with the outcomes of the FL approach across clients.

statistical_methods_used.method: Comparison of Identically Distributed (IID) and Non-Identically Distributed (Non-IID) datasets
statistical_methods_used.purpose: To analyze the impact of low-pixel images with non-identical distributions over clients and assess the effect of data distribution on model performance.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons between models and data distributions (IID vs Non-IID) were based on descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and loss, without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2019 Dataset (Baseline Transfer Lea.notes: EfficientNetB3 showed the best performance before augmentation with an accuracy of 87.86% and validation accuracy of 69.07%.

ISIC 2019 Dataset (Baseline Transfer Lea.metric: Accuracy, Validation Accuracy, Precision, Validation Precision, Recall, Validation Recall, Loss, Validation Loss
ISIC 2019 Dataset (Baseline Transfer Lea.notes: EfficientNetB3 achieved the highest accuracy (93.22%) and validation accuracy (90.33%) after augmentation. VITB16 had the lowest validation loss (0.51).

ISIC 2019 Dataset (Minimal Memory Models.notes: Results for minimal memory models at 224x224 resolution showed lower detection accuracy due to large image size relative to memory constraints.
EfficientNetV2S with 32x32x3 images achieved maximum accuracy of 91.67% among minimal memory models.

ISIC 2019 Dataset (Federated Learning -.notes: The paper reports an overall accuracy of 89.83% for IID and 90.64% for Non-IID in the abstract/conclusion, but Table 6 and Section 15 specify 98.17% for IID and 90.64% validation accuracy for Non-IID. The abstract's 89.83% likely refers to a different aggregation or specific client median, while the detailed results highlight 98.17% for IID. The conclusion states validation accuracy of 89.83% and 90.64% with IID and Non-IID respectively.

architecture_used.main_model: EfficientNetV2S (primary), EfficientNetB3, ResNet50, NasNetMobile, DenseNet201, MobileNetV2, VGG16, VITB16
architecture_used.pretraining: Pre-trained deep learning models used for transfer learning

architecture_details.hardware_environment: Windows 10 PC, 8 GB Google Drive storage

training_details.augmentation: Random and fixed rotation angles applied
training_details.optimization: Not explicitly reported (standard Keras/Tensorflow defaults implied)
training_details.loss_function: Categorical loss (implied by softmax layer and categorical losses mentioned)

classification_or_output_head.classes: 9 different forms of skin illnesses (specific class names not fully enumerated in architecture section, but SCC, BCC, MM, Nevus, Seborrhoeic Keratosis mentioned in context)

machine_learning_or_deep_learning_models: Multiple transfer learning models using deep learning
preprocessing_techniques: Resizing images to standard pixel sizes of 224 × 224 and 32 × 32
Data augmentation including horizontal flip, vertical flip, shear (35%), and zoom (35%)
Splitting dataset into training and testing subsets using an 80:20 ratio
Random selection and distribution of pre-processed data among N clients to simulate federated learning environment

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Both random and fixed rotation angles were applied.

conclusions: Lightweight federated transfer learning models, specifically EfficientNetV2S, can effectively classify skin cancer types using minimal resources and low-pixel images (32 × 32 × 3).

The proposed EfficientNetV2S model achieved high classification accuracy in a federated learning ecosystem, with 89.83% accuracy for identically distributed (IID) data and 90.64% accuracy for non-identically distributed (Non-IID) data.
Federated learning preserves patient data privacy by keeping sensitive data decentralized at local clients, reducing the risk of data breaches compared to centralized storage.
The study demonstrates that reducing image resolution to 32 × 32 × 3 allows for effective model training on minimal memory devices while maintaining high diagnostic accuracy.
Among baseline transfer learning models tested on 224 × 224 × 3 images, EfficientNetB3 showed superior performance before augmentation (87.86% accuracy) and after augmentation (93.22% accuracy).
The diagnostic challenge of distinguishing between similar lesions, such as nevus and melanoma, can be addressed by AI models, though visual similarities remain a clinical difficulty.
The proposed method offers a viable alternative for skin cancer detection in resource-constrained environments by balancing computational efficiency with diagnostic performance.
important_remarks: The study relies on a single dataset (ISIC 2019) without external validation on independent cohorts, limiting generalizability to other populations or imaging devices.
The paper explicitly acknowledges a limitation regarding the deployment of the proposed methodology in real-time ecosystems using mobile or handheld devices, noting this as future research.

There is a discrepancy in reported accuracy metrics: the abstract and conclusion state 89.83% for IID and 90.64% for Non-IID, while Table 6 and Section 15 report 98.17% accuracy for IID and 90.64% validation accuracy for Non-IID, creating ambiguity in the primary performance claim.
The research was conducted without external funding, which may indicate limited resources for extensive validation or broader dataset acquisition.

The use of low-resolution images (32 × 32 × 3) for the federated learning component significantly reduces pixel detail, which may impact the model's ability to detect subtle diagnostic features compared to standard 224 × 224 inputs, despite the claim of maintained accuracy.
No statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) was performed to validate the differences in performance between models or data distributions.
